notes.anchor: CASSIVS PROCOS. S. D. M. CICERONI
notes.note: C. Cassius Longinus again writes as PROCOS. (proconsul); S. D. is salutem dicit, "sends greetings," to M. Cicero. The trailing letters in the manuscript opener are corrupt.

notes.anchor: I forbade to be delivered until it had first been read to you
notes.note: Cassius routed his official dispatch to the Senate through Cicero, instructing his couriers to let Cicero read it first — a mark of how far he relied on Cicero to manage affairs at Rome.

notes.anchor: after Trebonius was foully murdered, seized Asia
notes.note: Dolabella tortured and killed C. Trebonius, the tyrannicide governor of Asia, at Smyrna in January 43 BC, then took over the province; the Senate declared him a public enemy.
"Brigands" (latrones) is the standard republican slur for Antony, Dolabella, and their partisans, denying them the standing of legitimate magistrates or soldiers.

notes.anchor: he would have held Apamea shut up until it was taken by storm
notes.note: Apamea on the Orontes, a strong city of Syria where the renegade Q. Caecilius Bassus had entrenched himself; his troops mutinied and came over to Cassius before it had to be stormed.

notes.anchor: The Nones of May, from the camp.
notes.note: The Nones of May = 7 May (43 BC). A postscript (§5) added news, received after the letter was drafted, that Dolabella had moved into Cilicia.
